description: Beyond-syllabus enrichment exercises for fast finishers. Topics not assessed in the ACP exam (recursion, unions, enums, random file access). Unlocked after CO1-CO5 are at Level 3.
department: School of Computer Science and Engineering
course: Advanced C Programming with Generative AI

co_descriptions.CO1: Develop modular and reusable code using functions (call by value and call by reference), arrays and structures.
co_descriptions.CO2: Apply modular programming to manage complex data collections using Arrays of Structures.
co_descriptions.CO3: Develop modular and reusable code using pointers and dynamic memory allocation.
co_descriptions.CO4: Develop modular and reusable code utilizing Strings by implementing user-defined logic for processing text-based data.
co_descriptions.CO5: Develop a modular application for file handling in C.
co_descriptions.CO6: Leverage generative AI to design and build a mini-project application.

RECUR.name: Recursion
RECUR.description: Recursive algorithms — not in the ACP syllabus. For advanced learners who have completed all CO1–CO5 topics.

exercises.title: Recursive factorial
exercises.concept_under_test: recursion, base case, recursive case, call stack
exercises.problem_statement: Write a recursive function long long factorial(int n) that returns n! (0 <= n <= 20). Identify and implement the base case explicitly.
constraints: Must be recursive (no loop in factorial)
Must have an explicit base case with a comment
mental_model_checkpoints: Can trace the call stack for n=3: factorial(3) → factorial(2) → factorial(1) → factorial(0)
Can explain what happens without a base case

common_mistakes.mistake: Missing or wrong base case — infinite recursion
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: When does your function stop calling itself? What is the smallest input that goes straight to a return value without another recursive call?

style_requirements: Comment identifying base case and recursive case

UNION.name: Unions
UNION.description: Unions — not in the ACP syllabus. For advanced learners.

exercises.title: Union memory sharing demonstration
exercises.concept_under_test: union definition, shared memory, sizeof union
exercises.problem_statement: Define a union Data with members: int i, float f, char str[20]. Print the size of the union. Store an int 65 in i, then read and print the value of str[0] (expect 'A'). Explain in a comment why this works.
exercises.sample_output:
Size of union: 20
As char: A

test_cases.label: standard
test_cases.expected_output:
Size of union: 20
As char: A

constraints: Must demonstrate value overlap via int and char member
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain why storing 65 in i makes str[0] == 'A' (ASCII)

common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If a struct has an int and a char[20], what is sizeof(struct)? If a union has the same members, what is sizeof(union)? Why the difference?

ENUM.name: Enumerations
ENUM.description: Enumerations — not in the ACP syllabus. For advanced learners.

exercises.title: Days of the week with enum
exercises.concept_under_test: enum definition, enum values, switch on enum
exercises.problem_statement: Define an enum Day (MON=1 through SUN=7). Read an integer 1-7. Use a switch to print the full day name. For invalid input, print 'Invalid day'.
constraints: Must use enum (not plain int constants)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state what value MON has and what WED has
Can explain why enum values can be used in switch

common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What happens in your switch if the user enters 9? What should happen?

FSEEK.name: Random File Access
FSEEK.description: fseek, ftell, rewind — not in the ACP syllabus. For advanced learners who want to explore random file access.

exercises.title: File size and reverse print using fseek and ftell
exercises.concept_under_test: fseek, ftell, SEEK_END, SEEK_SET, random access
exercises.problem_statement: Write a program that opens an existing text file 'sample.txt' (create it first with fprintf). Use fseek(fp, 0, SEEK_END) and ftell(fp) to determine its size. Then use fseek to navigate to specific byte positions and print the file size in bytes.
constraints: Use fseek and ftell (not stat or platform APIs)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain what SEEK_END means as the origin for fseek
Can state what ftell returns — byte offset from start of file

common_mistakes.mistake: Does not rewind before reading content after seeking to end
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: After fseek(fp, 0, SEEK_END), where is the file position? Can you read from that position?
